messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"som-window-cumulative-misread","title":"The 1% Year-1 anchor is a 36-month number wearing a 12-month label","narrative":"obtainableFactor's 1% rests on Prospeo's staged benchmark, read as an annual snapshot. But Prospeo itself frames SOM as revenue captured over 1-3 years, IdeaPlan says 'one to three years', Wise says 'three to five years', and TryBuildCo's own worked example lands at 0.1% of SAM — below the model's stated floor of 0.5%. The cross-check comparable (Tractian '$1.2M first year') is unverified against its own source, which shows $200K starting revenue. For a pre-launch industrial entrant with zero installed base and no certifications on day 0, the honest 12-month number is the bottom of the reference class, not its midpoint. The whole YAM figure is a period-mismatch error, not a market estimate.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Every source underlying the 1% benchmark defines the obtainable percentage as a cumulative 1-3 or 3-5 year capture window, not a Year-1 snapshot — the model pins a multi-year ratio to a 12-month YAM","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c1","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c5","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c6"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The two cross-checks that were supposed to independently confirm 1% both fail on inspection: TryBuildCo's worked example yields ~0.1% of SAM (below the model's 0.5% floor), and Tractian's revenue started at $200K — the '$1.2M first year' number cited in the derivation does not appear in the source","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c7","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c10"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor 0.01 overstates the honest pre-launch Year-1 capture by roughly 3x; the corrected value sits near 0.3% of SAM, and the sensitivity band's low edge (0.5%) does not even contain the reference-class outcomes","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","profile:Stage"]}],"whyMissable":"The node is marked 'triangulated' and the refinement cycle already 'caught' one benchmark misread (Year-2 read as Year-1), so the team believes the period question is settled. The surface appearance of a completed correction hides that the corrected source itself defines the percentage over a multi-year window — the fix repeated the same category of error one level down.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A documented comparable — an industrial hardware entrant into DC infrastructure — that captured ≥1% of a regional SAM in its first 12 months from commercial launch, with dated revenue evidence.","likelihood":{"value":0.55,"rationale":"The window-semantics mismatch is directly visible in the cited sources' own text (Prospeo, IdeaPlan, Wise all describe multi-year windows), and the Tractian cross-check number is unverifiable against its source — this is closer to a documented error than a speculative risk.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.003,"note":"Multi-year benchmark converted to an honest 12-month figure for a zero-installed-base entrant, consistent with TryBuildCo's 0.1% worked example and Tractian's actual $200K start"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Quote-to-PO conversion time in the first CE opportunities","where":"Own CRM pipeline reviewed at month 6; cross-referenced against TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) award timelines for rack-power line items","threshold":"Median cycle >9 months at month 6 implies Year-1 closes are structurally impossible at the 1% level","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Published first-year revenue disclosures of comparable DC-hardware entrants (Asian PDU makers entering EU)","where":"Company registries / Crunchbase / vendor press releases announcing first EU revenue milestones","threshold":"No comparable showing >0.5% regional SAM capture in year 1","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build a bottom-up pipeline model (named CE accounts × realistic win rate × cycle time × rack capacity) to replace the top-down benchmark before committing the Year-1 plan","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"The ledger's own promotionPath names this; doing it pre-launch converts a period-mismatched heuristic into a defensible number"},{"action":"Re-denominate Year-1 targets in design-wins and framework qualifications rather than revenue, and set the revenue plan on a 36-month SOM curve","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns internal expectations and investor communication with what the reference class actually delivers in 12 months"}]},{"id":"prelet-lockin-serviceable-window","title":"2026 demand is already specified: the serviceable haircut ignores that reachability has a clock","narrative":"serviceableFactor 0.55 is framed as a channel-and-regulatory reach haircut, as if reach were a static property. But for DC electrical gear, the demand that will invoice in the next 12 months was specified at design stage — the model's own cross-check admits 12-24 month procurement lock, and the corpus shows lock-in occurring far earlier (transformer lead times of 48-60 months, hyperscale cycles up to 4-7 years). Meanwhile the entrant's EU compliance stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, plus EN 50600-compatible documentation) consumes 6-9 months from day 0. The intersection of 'demand contestable in months 1-12' and 'entrant qualified to bid' is a fraction of the static 0.55 — the serviceable share within the YAM window is a different, smaller number than the serviceable share of the market in steady state.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Rack-power specifications for capacity landing in 2026 were locked at design/procurement stage well before the venture's launch date — the model's own cross-check concedes a 12-24 month lock window, and the growth the market shape describes is precisely this pre-committed pipeline","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","ledger:shape.cagr"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The entrant cannot bid even the contestable residue until its compliance stack clears: the full EU entry set (WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, RoHS, EORI) is broader than the ledger's named certs and runs 6-9 months, and EN 50600 facility-standard compatibility must be documented for operator RFQs","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c11","profile:Stage"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"serviceableFactor 0.55 counts demand that is temporally unreachable within the first 12 months; the effective serviceable share for the YAM window — demand both uncommitted and open to a newly-certified vendor — is closer to 0.35, and the node has no bottom-up cross-check to catch this","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c1"]}],"whyMissable":"The serviceable haircut looks handled because it already discounts 45% of the TAM and cites the regulatory stack. What the static number hides is a timing dimension: 0.55 may be right for the market in general and simultaneously wrong for every month of the venture's first year, because 'reachable' and 'reachable now' are different sets. A team reviewing the number checks its level, not its clock.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Three or more CE operator RFQs for rack PDUs in the next two quarters that (a) accept vendors whose certifications complete during the tender process and (b) award within 9 months of publication.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"Base rate for enterprise/DC hardware: sales cycles of 12-18 months and certification of 6-9 months are well-established for the reference class; the open question is only what fraction of CE demand is genuinely spot-contestable, which no source quantifies — the absence itself is the risk.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"set","value":0.35,"note":"Static reach haircut tightened to window-adjusted reach: demand that is both uncommitted and open to a vendor whose certification completes mid-year"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Time from RFQ publication to award for rack-power line items in CE","where":"TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) and major CE colo operator procurement portals (e.g., NTT, Vantage, Data4 supplier registration pages)","threshold":"Median award cycle >9 months, or supplier prequalification requiring completed certification at bid time","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Quoted lead time for the venture's own TÜV/CE certification slots","where":"TÜV NORD / TÜV SÜD test-lab booking confirmations and notified-body queue quotes","threshold":"Certification completion date beyond month 8 of the launch year","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a bottom-up channel-coverage model (named CE accounts × spec-lock status × certification-at-bid requirements) to produce a window-adjusted serviceable estimate","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"The ledger's promotionPath names this model; adding the spec-lock dimension is the marginal work that prices the clock"},{"action":"Start the full EU certification stack before launch and pre-register as a supplier on operator procurement portals so month-1 of the commercial window is not month-1 of the compliance clock","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts sequential cert→bid timeline into parallel; recovers 4-6 months of the window"}]},{"id":"framework-foreclosure-beachhead","title":"The chosen beachhead buys through global frameworks: half the large-operator cell is not a market for a regional entrant","narrative":"The venture's stated first channel is large operators, and the model gives that cell 40% of PDU spend on the logic 'large operators buy direct'. The corpus shows what 'direct' actually means at that scale: Digital Realty's $373M Schneider framework agreement, Iren's $1.6bn all-in Dell contract, PDU spend embedded as one line inside integrated rack/power/cooling packages. Combined with CR3 ≈ 0.55 among vendors who sell exactly those bundles, the buying event a new entrant needs — a discrete, multi-bidder PDU bake-off at a large operator — is disappearing. The only standalone PDU procurements found in the corpus were small, 1-2 bidder, sometimes explicitly sole-sourced. The beachhead the profile names is the cell most structurally closed to the venture; the model prices it as the biggest open door.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Large operators consolidate rack-power purchasing into multi-year global framework agreements and integrated vendor packages rather than discrete PDU line items — the exact procurement pattern documented in the cust.operator-large evidence trail","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","digest:cr3-vendors#c1"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"With the #1 vendor alone at 15.8% and a semi-consolidated top set (Schneider, Vertiv, Eaton, ABB, Legrand) selling bundled infrastructure, framework renewal — not open tender — becomes the default buying event; the residual standalone tenders observed were 1-2 bidder or sole-sourced ('No substitutions')","refs":["digest:cr3-vendors#c0","digest:cr3-vendors#c2","ledger:shape.cr3"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The 0.40 large-operator share — itself an unsourced internal estimate with no channel-split evidence — overstates the spend a regional entrant can actually contest; roughly half the cell is foreclosed behind frameworks the entrant cannot enter in year 1, and the venture's go-to-market points straight at it","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","profile:Product"]}],"whyMissable":"The founder's risk list treats 'large operators buy direct' as good news — direct means no distributor margin and a named buyer. The surface appearance of an accessible direct channel hides that 'direct' at hyperscale/large-colo means framework-direct: the door has a lock shaped like a 5-year global agreement, and the buyer's procurement team is contractually discouraged from running the bake-off the entrant's plan requires.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Two named CE large operators (e.g., a Frankfurt or Amsterdam colo/hyperscale buyer) confirming a vendor-neutral, multi-bidder rack-PDU RFQ process in 2026 that is open to suppliers outside existing framework agreements.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"Framework consolidation at large operators is directly evidenced (Digital Realty-Schneider, Iren-Dell cases in the node's own tension trail), and the customerType split it undermines is admitted to be an unsourced internal estimate; uncertainty is only over the foreclosed fraction, not the mechanism's existence.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"set","value":0.2,"note":"≈half of large-operator PDU spend sits inside global frameworks and integrated packages that a pre-launch regional entrant cannot contest; siblings deliberately not re-normalized — this is absolute foreclosure, not a mix shift"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"New or renewed multi-year power-infrastructure framework announcements by CE-active large operators","where":"Digital Realty / Equinix / Vantage / NTT press releases and Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton earnings-call order-book commentary","threshold":"≥2 new CE-relevant frameworks announced covering rack power","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Bidder counts and substitution clauses on standalone rack-PDU tenders","where":"TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) award notices and operator supplier portals","threshold":"Median bidders ≤2 or 'no substitution' language on >half of observed PDU line items","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run the three CE operator/distributor procurement interviews the ledger already flags as pending, specifically instrumenting for framework coverage vs open-RFQ share of PDU spend","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Directly de-risks the largest unsourced cell in the funnel; the interview guide should ask 'when did you last run a competitive PDU bake-off' not 'who do you buy from'"},{"action":"Re-sequence the beachhead to mid-size operators and enterprise retrofits, where discrete PDU purchasing survives, and approach large operators via second-source qualification inside existing frameworks rather than head-on displacement","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Second-source slots are the documented entry path for challengers in framework-dominated categories"}]},{"id":"channel-calendar-lockout","title":"Distributor line-cards and integrator qualification run on annual calendars the 12-month plan cannot intersect","narrative":"35% of the modeled funnel flows through OEM/integrator (0.22) and distributor (0.13) cells — both admitted internal estimates with zero evidence attached. The reference class says both channels have a calendar: distributor line-card reviews are annual events requiring certifications, stock commitments and demand proof in hand; integrators demonstrably select branded incumbents on certification, DCIM compatibility and support (every case study found — Vertiv/Greenergy, Legrand/MIND Park — shows brand selection, none shows a new entrant winning a slot). A pre-launch vendor with no certs on day 0 misses the current listing cycle entirely and enters the next one as an unproven applicant. Within the YAM window these two cells are not small — they are closed, and the model treats 'closed this year' as indistinguishable from 'open at 35%'.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["cust.oem","cust.distributor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Distributor line-card onboarding is an annual-cycle gate requiring completed certifications and reference installs at application time — assets a pre-launch entrant does not possess in month 1 and cannot assemble before the cycle closes","refs":["ledger:cust.distributor","profile:Stage","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The parallel OEM/integrator path is gated the same way but on brand: integrators select PDU suppliers on certification, DCIM compatibility and support track record, a pattern consistent across the corpus's integrator case studies and the incumbent vendor sets that dominate every published landscape","refs":["ledger:cust.oem","digest:cr3-vendors#c4","digest:cr3-vendors#c7"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"cust.oem (0.22) and cust.distributor (0.13) together carry 35% of the funnel into SAM, but within the first 12 months both cells are calendar-foreclosed; the SAM the Year-1 obtainable percentage is applied to is materially smaller than modeled","refs":["ledger:cust.oem","ledger:cust.distributor"]}],"whyMissable":"Channel shares look like a mix question — 'we'll get some of each' — so the risk review debates the percentages, not their availability. The hidden feature is that indirect channels are not continuously open markets: they are gated queues with annual admission, and a pre-launch company applies to the queue rather than selling into the cell. Nothing in a static share number signals that the cell has an opening date.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A signed CE master-distributor listing or OEM/integrator supply agreement with committed volumes, effective within the venture's first six months.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Annual line-card cycles and brand-based integrator selection are standard for the reference class (industrial electrical hardware entering EU distribution); uncertainty is whether an aggressive pre-launch certification push can compress one cycle, which some Asian PDU entrants have managed with EU-stock commitments.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.distributor","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Distributor cell unavailable within the YAM window — listing cycle cannot complete inside 12 months from a zero-cert start"},{"nodeId":"cust.oem","op":"set","value":0.11,"note":"Integrator cell half-foreclosed: brand/certification selection excludes the entrant from established integrator programs; residual is opportunistic white-label volume"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Line-card additions in rack-power categories at CE electrical/IT distributors","where":"Rexel, Sonepar, Also/Ingram product-catalog update announcements and new-vendor press releases","threshold":"Zero new rack-PDU vendors added in the trailing two quarters signals a hard-gated cycle","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Integrator RFI/qualification requirements for PDU suppliers","where":"Vendor-qualification questionnaires from CE rack integrators and modular-DC builders (e.g., responses to the venture's own outreach, logged monthly)","threshold":"Certification-complete and ≥2 reference installs required at application in >75% of responses","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Interview three CE distributors/integrators now (the ledger's own pending evidence) to map listing-cycle dates, entry requirements, and whether a 'new vendor' fast-track exists","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.oem","note":"Simultaneously prices the unsourced 0.22/0.13 cells and establishes whether the calendar can be intersected at all in year 1"},{"action":"Concentrate Year-1 entirely on the direct mid-operator/enterprise cells and treat channel listings as a Year-2 milestone with pre-work (cert dossier, EU stock plan) started in month 1","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Stops the plan from booking revenue from cells that have no opening date inside the window"}]},{"id":"poc-acceptance-reversal","title":"The industrial-hardware acceptance base rate un-books a third of whatever Year-1 obtains","narrative":"Whatever tiny share the venture obtains in year 1 will be obtained as pilots and first deployments — the profile's own 'conservative ramp' guarantees it. The reference class puts a number on what happens next: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems are never fully accepted — customers sign, the vendor deploys, and six months later the gear comes back out. Intelligent PDUs are acutely exposed because acceptance hinges on post-deployment behavior: DCIM integration, metering accuracy, firmware stability under the operator's monitoring stack. The model's obtainableFactor is a gross booking rate; nothing in the funnel converts it to net-of-reversal, and for a first-year entrant the reversal doesn't just cut revenue — it destroys the reference accounts that the Year-2 3% benchmark silently assumes exist.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"30% of deployed industrial hardware systems in the reference class were never fully accepted and required uninstallation — a measured base rate, not an anecdote","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c12"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"A pre-launch entrant's first-12-month wins are structurally pilots/POCs with acceptance clauses; intelligent-PDU acceptance depends on DCIM compatibility and metering behavior that only surfaces after deployment, in mid/enterprise accounts where the venture has no support track record","refs":["profile:Stage","ledger:cust.operator-mid","digest:cr3-vendors#c4"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor 0.01 is a gross capture rate; net of the acceptance-failure base rate, realized Year-1 share runs ~30% lower, and the staged benchmark's Year-2 step (3%) presumes surviving reference accounts that reversals eliminate","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c0"]}],"whyMissable":"Acceptance risk hides inside a revenue number that looks conservative already — at 1% of SAM, no one audits whether the 1% stays booked. The surface appearance of prudence (a small number) conceals that the number is gross, and the funnel has no node where post-deployment reversal could even be expressed.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"The venture's first three CE pilot deployments passing formal customer acceptance within 90 days of install, with no rollback or return clause exercised in the following two quarters.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"The 30% non-acceptance figure is a single-source base rate from adjacent industrial hardware, not PDU-specific; PDUs are simpler than full industrial systems, but DCIM-integration acceptance criteria and zero support track record cut the other way.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.7,"note":"Gross Year-1 capture haircut by the industrial-hardware non-acceptance base rate (~30% of deployments reverse)"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Acceptance and return-rights clauses in pilot contracts","where":"Redlines from CE operator procurement teams on the venture's pilot MSAs, reviewed at each signing","threshold":"Acceptance windows >90 days or unconditional return rights in a majority of pilot contracts","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"DCIM interoperability status of the product against the platforms CE operators run","where":"Sunbird/Schneider EcoStruxure/Vertiv Trellis compatibility matrices and the venture's integration test reports","threshold":"Product absent from ≥2 of the major DCIM compatibility lists at launch","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run a structured pre-launch DCIM interoperability certification program (top-3 platforms used by CE operators) and publish the compatibility matrix before the first pilot ships","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Removes the single largest post-deployment acceptance failure mode before it can trigger the base rate"},{"action":"Instrument the first five pilots as an acceptance-rate study — formal acceptance criteria, 90-day gates, root-cause logging on any reversal — to replace the generic 30% base rate with a venture-specific one","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Converts the obtainableFactor from a benchmark guess to a measured pipeline parameter, the ledger's own stated promotion path"}]}]}
